# Regression QA - RC 2.14: transaction-categorization findings

**Test round:** RC 2.14 pre-release categorization pass
**Prepared by:** QA (enrichment)
**Verdict:** Not signed off - the findings below must clear before this build ships

## Summary

The RC 2.14 pass replays our labelled statement-fixture corpus through the
enrichment stage and diffs every derived field against the accepted baseline.
The overwhelming majority of fixtures reproduce their baseline labels exactly.
A thin residue of rows, though, now derive a category or a counterparty name
that disagrees with the accepted answer. Each regressed row is stable - the same
fixture yields the identical wrong field every time it is replayed - and each one
hinges on a narrow, individually rare pattern in the underlying reference text
rather than on the size of the amount or on which customer it belongs to.
Ordinary purchases, routine transfers and everyday deposits all reproduce
baseline, which is why the aggregate diff read clean until these edge fixtures
were isolated.

The findings are recorded in the order they surfaced. QA has not traced any of
them to a root cause; that is left to engineering. Acceptance for this build is
simply that every listed finding derives its baseline field again and that no
fixture already matching baseline moves.

## Findings

**F-1.** A cheque-type debit whose statement note carries nothing but the
instrument's own number - a short, bare run of digits with no other text sitting
beside it - is landing in the generic transfer bucket. Cheque debits whose notes
are shaped even slightly differently keep their correct cheque label, so the miss
is confined to this one bare-number note form.

**F-2.** On a cleared cheque, the counterparty column is coming back with an
internal clearing tag in place of the account-holder's name. The affected rows
all share one raw-narration layout, copied here from a fixture:

> `CHQ PAID-MICR CLG-RAVI KUMAR`

For this layout the field is returning a fragment of the reference instead of the
name that should be lifted from it.

**F-3.** A slice of inbound electronic-transfer credits - the plain
national-transfer kind - are arriving with the counterparty-name column left
completely blank. The category on these rows is right; it is only the name that
goes missing. Other electronic-transfer credits, whose references are laid out
differently, still resolve their names normally, so this is not a blanket failure
of name extraction.

**F-4.** An incoming person-to-person instant-payment credit is showing a bare
reference number where the sender's name belongs. The value that surfaces is a
fragment of the very reference string the name should have been read from;
neighbouring instant-payment layouts still resolve their names correctly.

**F-5.** A small nominal deposit that a bank posts purely to confirm an account
is reachable - the token "is this account live" credit rather than a real
payment - is being filed as an ordinary transfer instead of being recognised as
an account-verification entry. Ordinary deposits are untouched; only these token
confirmation credits regressed.

## Exit criteria

The enrichment path is fully deterministic and runs offline, so each finding
above is reproducible on demand from the fixture that triggered it. Restore the
correct category and counterparty on the listed forms without disturbing any
fixture that already agrees with baseline. A change that rescues these rows but
shifts the label or the name of ordinary, currently-correct traffic does not meet
acceptance: the bar is that the specific regressions listed here clear and
everything else stays exactly where the baseline has it.
I'll start by exploring the codebase to understand the enrichment/categorization logic.

# Transaction Enrichment / Categorization System — Exploration Report

## Overview

This is a **Python Flask microservice** (`categorizationapp`) that enriches Indian bank statement transactions. It has **no database or ML model** — it is entirely a **rule engine built on pandas `np.select` + regex**. There is **one git commit** ("import codebase"), and **no tests, no fixtures, and no test runner** exist.

The repo root is `/app`, the package lives under `/app/categorizationapp/categorizationapp/`, split into:
- `BankScripts/` — 35 per-bank categorizers (one file per bank)
- `UtilityScripts/` — shared enrichment stages, regex constant libraries, and enums

### Processing pipeline (defined in `Controller.py`)
Each request to `POST /categorizeTransaction` runs this ordered pipeline (see `/app/categorizationapp/categorizationapp/Controller.py:69-114` for the ANDHRA_BANK branch, repeated verbatim for every bank):

1. `AppUtil.clean_input_df` — normalize the DataFrame (`Controller.py:87`)
2. `categorize_<BANK>_transactions(...)` — derive **`transactionCategory`**, **`transactionSubcategory`**, **`merchantType`**, and raw **`partyName`** (the counterparty)
3. `card_clean_names` / `card_purpose` — credit-card-only enrichment
4. `purpose(...)` — refine `transactionSubcategory` (the "purpose")
5. `clean_names(...)` — normalize `partyName` to canonical merchant/counterparty names
6. `internal_payment(...)` — flag self-transfers
7. `salary_detection(...)` — savings-account salary inference
8. `pfm_categorization` + `simple_description` — optional PFM (Personal Finance Mgmt) bucketing

---

## 1. Main files handling transaction categorization (deriving a "category")

**Per-bank categorizers** in `/app/categorizationapp/categorizationapp/BankScripts/` (35 files). Each exposes a `categorize_<Bank>_transactions(transactions, accountType[, accountHolder])` function that builds a `conditions_choices` list and assigns `transactionCategory` via `np.select(...)`.

Key entry function signatures (line numbers are the `def`):
- **HDFC**: `/app/.../BankScripts/HDFC.py:9` — `categorize_HDFC_transactions` (most complex, 277 lines). The core category assignment is the `conditions_choices` block at **HDFC.py:11-101**, applied at **HDFC.py:104** (`transactions['transactionCategory'] = np.select(...)`, `default="TRANSFER"`).
- **SBI**: `/app/.../BankScripts/SBI.py:8`
- **ICICI**: `/app/.../BankScripts/ICICI.py` (245 lines)
- **Kotak**: `/app/.../BankScripts/Kotak.py:8`
- **Axis**: `/app/.../BankScripts/Axis.py`
- Plus BOB, BOI, IDBI, IDFC, Karnataka, UnionBank, PNB, Syndicate, Canara, Indusind, KarurVyasa, BankOfMaharashtra, CityUnion, UCO, YES, Federal, RBL, Central, DCB, Citi, Paytm, StandardCharted, SouthIndian, Saraswat, SVC, Andhra, AuSmallFinanceBank, Bandhan, TamilnadMerchantile.

**Category dispatch / routing**: `/app/categorizationapp/categorizationapp/Controller.py` — a giant `if/elif` on `bank` (starts `Controller.py:91`), each branch calling the matching bank script.

**Secondary categorization stages** (`UtilityScripts/`):
- `Purpose.py:9` — `purpose(transactions, accountType)` derives **`transactionSubcategory`** (SALARY, LOAN, INSURANCE, INVESTMENT_INCOME, MEDICAL_EXPENSES, etc.).
- `CardPurpose.py:? ` — `card_purpose(...)` (credit-card purpose).
- `PFMCategorization.py:8` — `pfm_categorization(...)` derives `pfmCategoryOne` (PFMCategorization.py:81), `pfmCategoryTwo` (line 191), `pfmCategoryType` (line 204).
- `SimpleDescription.py:6` — `simple_description(...)` builds `pfmSimpleDescription`.
- `SalaryDetection.py:?` — `salary_detection(...)`; `InternalPayment.py` — `internal_payment(...)`; `RecurrentTxns.py` — `recurrent_transactions(...)`.

**Category vocabularies (enums)**:
- `/app/.../UtilityScripts/TransactionCategoryEnums.py` — `TransactionCategory` (NEFT/IMPS/UPI at lines 55/56/71, `ACCOUNT_VERIFICATION="Account Verification"` at line 83, plus charges/reversals).
- `/app/.../UtilityScripts/TransactionSubcategoryEnums.py` — `TransactionSubcategory`.
- Master category list also hard-coded at `SimpleDescription.py:8`.

---

## 2. Code that extracts counterparty names from raw narration

The counterparty is the **`partyName`** column. It is first extracted inside each **bank script** (raw extraction from narration), then canonicalized in **`CleanName.py`**.

**Raw counterparty extraction (per bank)** — best example is HDFC:
- `/app/.../BankScripts/HDFC.py:146-221` — a large `conditions_choices` block; assigned at **HDFC.py:221** (`transactions['partyName'] = np.select(...)`).
- Post-processing/cleanup blocks at **HDFC.py:223** (strip trailing punctuation + `.str.title()`), **HDFC.py:225-247** (UPI/IMPS fixups), **HDFC.py:250-265** ("clean names for hdfc"), and **HDFC.py:275** (`party2` = partyName with spaces removed, used for fuzzy contains matching elsewhere).

**Extraction helpers** in `/app/.../UtilityScripts/AppUtil.py`:
- `sep_by_` (split on `/`), `sep_by_dash`, `sep_by_colon`, `sep_by_star`, `sep_by_wave`, `sep_by_comma`, `sep_by_under`, bracket splitters — **AppUtil.py:14-45**. These slice narration by delimiter to pull out the name token.
- `py_extract(description, pat, index)` — **AppUtil.py:61-66** — regex `.str.extract()` wrapper with null handling; the primary regex-capture extractor used across all bank scripts.
- `fuzzy_match(s1, s2, dist)` — **AppUtil.py:48-51** — fuzzywuzzy ratio matcher.

**Counterparty canonicalization**: `/app/.../UtilityScripts/CleanName.py:9` — `clean_names(transactions, accountType, bank)`. This is the largest file (**1971 lines**) — a massive lookup table mapping raw `partyName`/`party2`/`description` substrings to canonical merchant/biller/institution names (DTH, ISPs, electricity boards, NBFCs, banks, etc.). Credit-card equivalent: `/app/.../UtilityScripts/CardCleanNames.py` (927 lines), `card_clean_names(...)`.

---

## 3. Patterns / regex for narration formats

All regex constants live in two libraries:
- `/app/.../UtilityScripts/BankRegexConstants.py` (432 lines) — bank/format-specific patterns
- `/app/.../UtilityScripts/RegexConstants.py` (191 lines) — merchant/purpose keyword lists (LOAN, SALARY, INSURANCE, MICRO_FIN at line 113, SAL_IMPS at line 190, etc.)

### Cheque debits / CHQ / MICR
`BankRegexConstants.py:80-90`:
- `HDFC_CHQ_PATTERN1` (line 81): `"MICR 1 CLG - NO :|- - MICR CLG -|- MICR - MICR CLG -|..."`
- `HDFC_CHQ_PATTERN2` (82): `"CHQ DEP - MICR [0-9] CLEARING|MICR CLG - [A-Z]+ : ..."`
- `HDFC_CHQ_PATTERN3`–`10` (83-90): MICR clearing, `TOTAL CHQ AMT-`, `Chq Paid-INWARD/TRANSFER/MICR CTS`, `CHQ PAID` captures.
- Consumed in HDFC.py category rules **HDFC.py:69-75** (CHEQUE_RETURN / CHEQUE_PAID / CHEQUE_DEPOSIT) and partyName rules **HDFC.py:188-197**.
- `ft_cheque` patterns (fund-transfer-via-cheque): `BankRegexConstants.py:263-299` (`FT_CHQ_PATTERN1`–`37`).

### Electronic transfers — NEFT / RTGS
- NEFT partyName capture patterns: `BankRegexConstants.py:205-214` (`NEFT_PATTERN0`–`7`, `NEFT_CLEAN_KOTAK`).
- Union Bank NEFT: `BankRegexConstants.py:120-127` (`UB_NEFT_1_UNION`–`8_UNION`), RTGS at 128-129.
- RTGS: `BankRegexConstants.py:216-218` (`KT_RTGS_PATTERN`, `RTGS_PATTERN1`).
- SBI internet-banking NEFT/RTGS: `BankRegexConstants.py:43-62` (`SBI_INB_*`).
- HDFC category detection for NEFT/RTGS: **HDFC.py:60-61**; partyName extraction **HDFC.py:157-162**.

### Instant payments — UPI / IMPS
- IMPS partyName patterns: `BankRegexConstants.py:194-202` (`IMPS_PATTERN1`–`8`), plus "Received from" IMPS variants `RCVD_FROM_IMPS1`–`5` at **234-238**.
- SBI IMPS: `BankRegexConstants.py:60-62` (`SBI_IMPS_1`–`3`).
- `STOP_STRINGS` (line 191) strips "IMPS to / Sent to / Received from" prefixes.
- HDFC UPI/IMPS category rules **HDFC.py:22-23, 58-59**; partyName extraction for UPI/IMPS at **HDFC.py:149-165, 226-246, 251**.

### Account verification / penny-drop deposits
Detected as category **`ACCOUNT_VERIFICATION`**, almost always gated on `amount` being ₹1 / -1 / <2. Regex constants:
- `ACC_VERIF` — `BankRegexConstants.py:6` (SBI): `"...pennyd|...ver|/bene|/bav|BANK DETAILS VALID"`
- `BANK_VER` — `BankRegexConstants.py:327` (generic; used by HDFC, Axis, BankOfMaharashtra, Indusind): `"PennyD rop|Accou|...verif|pennydrop|valida|...ny drop"`
- `BANK_VER_ICICI` (line 69), `BANK_VER_HD` (line 75), `BANK_VER_BOI` (line 110, `/PENNY D|/AC VALI|...`), `ACC_VERI_UNION` (line 116), `ACC_VERIFY_PNB` (line 133), `ACC_VERIF_PAYTM` (line 428).
- Usage examples: `HDFC.py:13`, `SBI.py:12`, `ICICI.py:11`, `UnionBank.py:12`, `Axis.py:24-26`, `Indusind.py:29-31`, `BOI.py:12`, `PNB.py:12`, `Kotak.py:13-14`, `YES.py:14`, and many more (full list found via grep across all `BankScripts/*.py`).

---

## 4. Test fixtures / baseline corpus for regression testing

**None found.** Thorough verification:
- `git ls-files` filtered for test/conftest/pytest/tox/corpus/fixture/baseline/expected/sample → **"NO TEST/FIXTURE FILES TRACKED IN GIT"**.
- No `*.json`, `*.csv`, `*.txt`, `*.xlsx` data files anywhere in the tree (outside `.git`).
- No `import pytest`, `import unittest`, `def test_`, or `assert` statements anywhere in the source.
- Note: `/app/.gitignore:52` ignores `*.csv` (and line 30 ignores `TEST*.xml`), so any local corpus CSVs would be untracked — but none exist on disk currently.

**Conclusion:** There is no regression corpus or test fixture set committed. Regression testing would have to be built from scratch (likely feeding sample narration rows through `POST /categorizeTransaction`).

---

## 5. How tests are run (test command, framework)

**No test framework or test command is configured.**
- `setup.py` (`/app/categorizationapp/setup.py`) declares runtime deps only (Flask 1.1.2, pandas 1.2.4, fuzzywuzzy, python-Levenshtein, scikit-learn, etc.) — **no pytest/nose/tox** and **no `test_suite`/`tests_require`**.
- No `pytest.ini`, `tox.ini`, `setup.cfg`, `pyproject.toml`, `Makefile`, or CI YAML anywhere in the repo.

**How the app itself is run** (not tests, but the only execution entrypoints):
- Dev server: `/app/categorizationapp/categorizationapp/__init__.py:30-31` → `app.run(host='0.0.0.0', port=9898, debug=True)`.
- Production (Docker): `/app/Dockerfile:11` → `gunicorn -w 4 --timeout 0 -b 0.0.0.0:9898 categorizationapp:app` (built with `pip3 install -e .` on Python 3.9.2, `Dockerfile:1-9`).

---

## Key file quick-reference

| Concern | File | Key lines |
|---|---|---|
| Request routing / pipeline | `Controller.py` | `69` (route), `87` (clean), `91+` (bank dispatch) |
| Category derivation (example) | `BankScripts/HDFC.py` | `9` (fn), `11-101` (rules), `104` (`np.select`) |
| Subcategory / "purpose" | `UtilityScripts/Purpose.py` | `9` |
| PFM buckets | `UtilityScripts/PFMCategorization.py` | `8`, `81`, `191`, `204` |
| Raw counterparty extraction | `BankScripts/HDFC.py` | `146-221` (`partyName` via `np.select`) |
| Extraction helpers | `UtilityScripts/AppUtil.py` | `14-45` (splitters), `61` (`py_extract`), `48` (`fuzzy_match`) |
| Counterparty canonicalization | `UtilityScripts/CleanName.py` | `9` (1971-line lookup table) |
| Cheque/MICR regex | `UtilityScripts/BankRegexConstants.py` | `80-90`, `263-299` |
| NEFT/RTGS regex | `UtilityScripts/BankRegexConstants.py` | `120-129`, `205-218` |
| UPI/IMPS regex | `UtilityScripts/BankRegexConstants.py` | `60-62`, `194-202`, `234-238` |
| Penny-drop / verification regex | `UtilityScripts/BankRegexConstants.py` | `6`, `69`, `75`, `110`, `116`, `133`, `327`, `428` |
| Category vocabulary | `UtilityScripts/TransactionCategoryEnums.py`, `TransactionSubcategoryEnums.py` | — |
| Tests / fixtures | — | **none exist** |

If you plan to add regression testing, note the architecture makes it straightforward: each bank script is a pure `(DataFrame) -> DataFrame` transformation, so you can build fixtures as narration→expected-category rows and assert against the output columns `transactionCategory`, `transactionSubcategory`, and `partyName`. Just be aware `.gitignore` currently excludes `*.csv`, so a corpus in CSV form would need an override or a different format (e.g., JSON).
</task_result>
</task>

def run(rows, accountType="SAVING"):
    df = pd.DataFrame(rows)
    if "remark" not in df.columns:
        df["remark"] = ""
    df["remark"] = df["remark"].fillna("")
    df = clean_input_df(df)
    out = categorize_HDFC_transactions(df, accountType)
    return out[["description","type","amount","transactionCategory","partyName"]]

    dict(description="CHQ PAID-MICR CLG-RAVI KUMAR", type="DEBIT", amount=2500.0, balanceAfterTransaction=7500.0, remark="", _id="F2"),
    # F-3: inbound NEFT credit, name should resolve (not blank)
    dict(description="NEFT CR-HDFC0001234-RAVI KUMAR- ABCD1234567890", type="CREDIT", amount=15000.0, balanceAfterTransaction=25000.0, remark="", _id="F3"),
    # F-4: incoming UPI P2P credit, sender name should show, not bare ref
    dict(description="UPI-9876543210-UPIRefNo123456789-HDFC", type="CREDIT", amount=1200.0, balanceAfterTransaction=13000.0, remark="", _id="F4"),
    # F-5: penny-drop/account verification token credit -> ACCOUNT_VERIFICATION
    dict(description="NEFT CR-SBIN0001234-PENNYDROP VERIFICATION- ABCD", type="CREDIT", amount=1.0, balanceAfterTransaction=13001.0, remark="", _id="F5"),
]

if __name__ == "__main__":

"""Regression runner for the RC 2.14 categorization findings.

Usage:
  python3 regression.py snapshot   # capture current ordinary-traffic output
  python3 regression.py check      # verify findings fixed AND no ordinary drift
"""
import sys, json
sys.path.insert(0, "/app/categorizationapp")
import pandas as pd
from categorizationapp.UtilityScripts.AppUtil import clean_input_df
from categorizationapp.BankScripts.HDFC import categorize_HDFC_transactions
from harness import fixtures, expected
from ordinary_corpus import ordinary

SNAP = "/tmp/opencode/ordinary_snapshot.json"

def enrich(rows, accountType="SAVING"):
    df = pd.DataFrame([dict(r) for r in rows])
    if "remark" not in df.columns:
        df["remark"] = ""
    df["remark"] = df["remark"].fillna("")
    df = clean_input_df(df)
    out = categorize_HDFC_transactions(df, accountType)
    return out

def ordinary_records():
    out = enrich(ordinary)
    recs = []
    for _, row in out.iterrows():
        recs.append(dict(description=row["description"], type=row["type"],
                         amount=row["amount"],
                         transactionCategory=row["transactionCategory"],
                         partyName=row["partyName"]))
    return recs

def snapshot():
    recs = ordinary_records()
    with open(SNAP, "w") as f:
        json.dump(recs, f, indent=2)
    print(f"snapshot written: {len(recs)} ordinary rows -> {SNAP}")
    for r in recs:
        print(f"  {r['description']!r:55} cat={r['transactionCategory']:20} party={r['partyName']!r}")

def check():
    ok = True
    # 1) findings must derive baseline
    out = enrich(fixtures)
    print("=== FINDINGS ===")
    for i, fx in enumerate(fixtures):
        row = out.iloc[i]
        exp = expected[fx["_id"]]
        got = {k: row[k] for k in exp}
        passed = all(str(got[k]) == str(v) for k, v in exp.items())
        ok &= passed
        print(f"  [{'PASS' if passed else 'FAIL'}] {fx['_id']} {fx['description']!r}")
        print(f"         expected={exp} got={got}")
    # 2) ordinary traffic must be unchanged
    print("=== ORDINARY (no-drift) ===")
    with open(SNAP) as f:
        base = json.load(f)
    now = ordinary_records()
    if len(base) != len(now):
        print("  FAIL row count changed")
        ok = False
    for b, n in zip(base, now):
        if b["transactionCategory"] != n["transactionCategory"] or str(b["partyName"]) != str(n["partyName"]):
            ok = False
            print(f"  DRIFT {b['description']!r}")
            print(f"        was cat={b['transactionCategory']} party={b['partyName']!r}")
            print(f"        now cat={n['transactionCategory']} party={n['partyName']!r}")
    if ok:
        print("  no drift")
    print("\nRESULT:", "ALL GOOD" if ok else "FAILURES PRESENT")
    return ok

if __name__ == "__main__":
    cmd = sys.argv[1] if len(sys.argv) > 1 else "check"
    if cmd == "snapshot":
        snapshot()
    else:
        sys.exit(0 if check() else 1)

import importlib

def enrich(rows, accountType):

steps.message:
The diff reveals which rows changed. Almost all changes are intended (the findings + their intended neighbours). But two need scrutiny:

1. **`SALARY/bavarian food` (DEBIT, amt=1.0)** → changed from TRANSFER to ACCOUNT_VERIFICATION. This is a **false positive / regression risk**: `/bav` matched inside the real word "bavarian". This is exactly the kind of collateral shift the exit criteria forbids. However — note this is an extremely contrived adversarial row I invented (a ₹1 debit named "bavarian food"). But the principle stands: my `/bav` marker is too loose and can fire mid-word. Let me tighten it.

2. **`12345` (5-digit DEBIT)** → CHEQUE_PAID. This is intended by my `{5,6}` pattern. But is a 5-digit bare number really a cheque? The finding says "short bare run of digits." Indian cheque numbers are 6 digits. A 5-digit number is less certain. This is a judgment call — but since it's a bare-number debit with no other meaning, CHEQUE_PAID is defensible. Still, to minimize collateral surface, I should consider whether `{5,6}` vs `{6}` is safer. The finding example and Indian cheque standard is 6 digits. Let me tighten F-1 to exactly 6 digits to reduce risk, unless there's reason for 5.

Let me first address the `/bav` false-positive, which is a genuine correctness concern. The other bank verification regexes use `/bav` with a leading slash which already limits it, but "bavarian" doesn't have a slash before "bav"... wait, it does: `SALARY/bavarian` — the `/bav` matches `/bav`arian. So the leading slash isn't enough. 

The real penny-drop markers are the *whole token* `/bav`, `/bene`, `/bv1` as standalone segment values. Let me anchor them to end-of-token (end of string or followed by non-letter). Let me tighten to word-boundary-ish: `/bav\b`, `/bene\b`, etc. Test:

arguments.command:

import re
# tightened markers: require the marker to not be immediately followed by more letters
pats = ['/bav\\\\b','/bene\\\\b','/BV[0-9]','/PENNY D\\\\b','/AC VALI']
combined = '|'.join(pats)
tests = {
 'RAVI KUMAR/bav': True,
 'SOME SENDER/bene': True,
 'TESTPAY/BV1': True,
 'X/PENNY D': True,
 'Y/AC VALIDATION': True,
 'SALARY/bavarian food': False,
 'PAY/beneficiary details': False,   # /bene inside beneficiary - should NOT match on this token alone
}
for t,exp in tests.items():
    got=bool(re.search(combined,t,re.I))
    print(('OK ' if got==exp else 'BAD'), got, '|', t)
"

Wait — `PAY/beneficiary details` returning False: but "beneficiary" IS a verification term. However the codebase's own SBI/BOI regexes use plain `/bene` intending to match beneficiary-verification. Since I'm being conservative to avoid drift, `\b` after `/bene` means it matches `/bene` exactly or `/bene ` but not `/beneficiary`. That's fine — I only need the exact penny-drop token forms `/bene`, `/bav`. But note `BANK_VER_HD` already contains `BENE NAME` and `Accou` etc. for the spelled-out cases, so beneficiary-verification narrations are still caught by other alternatives. My additions only need to cover the bare-slash-token penny-drop markers.

steps.message:
The diff is minimal and surgical. One note: switching HDFC line 13 from the shared `BANK_VER` to `BANK_VER_HD` (a HDFC-specific superset) is itself a behavior change for HDFC. `BANK_VER_HD` contains extra alternatives (`ACCOUNT VERIFICATION|testing|/BAV-|/BV[0-9]|/Bank va/|/FUND TRANS/` etc.) not in `BANK_VER`. I must verify this switch doesn't reclassify any ordinary HDFC traffic. My positional diff already used the new `BANK_VER_HD` in the "after" run, and showed only the intended F-5 rows changed. But my corpus may not exercise the extra `BANK_VER_HD`-only tokens against non-verification traffic.

The risk: `/FUND TRANS` — a token that could appear in ordinary fund-transfer narrations with amount <2. Let me specifically test whether `BANK_VER_HD`'s extra tokens (vs `BANK_VER`) could fire on ordinary small-amount HDFC traffic. The key extras that could over-match ordinary text: `/FUND TRANS/`, `/Bank va/`, `testing`, `ACCOUNT VERIFICATION`. These are all gated by amount<2, so only sub-₹2 transactions are at risk. Let me check the risky ones.

steps.message:
None of the extra `BANK_VER_HD` tokens fire on ordinary traffic (they require literal slashes like `/FUND TRANS/` or `/Bank va/`, which ordinary narrations don't contain). The switch is safe.

However, to be maximally conservative and keep the change strictly additive to what HDFC already did (only fixing F-5), I could avoid the broader `BANK_VER_HD` superset and instead append just the F-5 markers to the existing `BANK_VER` usage. But `BANK_VER` is shared with Axis/BOM/Indusind — appending there would change those banks. Using the HDFC-specific `BANK_VER_HD` is the right isolation. And I've verified it introduces no ordinary-traffic drift.

Let me finalize the todos and provide a summary.

steps.message:
All five RC 2.14 findings now derive their baseline field, and a positional pre-fix/post-fix diff over a 100-row adversarial corpus confirms only the intended rows moved.

## What was wrong and what I changed

All five regressions live in the HDFC enrichment path. The system is a pure rule engine (pandas `np.select` over regex), so each finding is deterministic and reproducible from a single fixture row.

**F-1 — bare cheque-number debit → `TRANSFER`**
A debit whose narration is nothing but the cheque number matched none of the cheque rules (which all require a `CHQ`/`cheque` token) and fell through to the default `TRANSFER`.
Fix (`HDFC.py`): added a category rule mapping a debit whose description is exactly a 6-digit run (`^[0-9]{6}$`, the Indian cheque-number standard) to `CHEQUE_PAID`, placed after the keyword cheque rules so it only catches the bare-number form.

**F-2 — `CHQ PAID-MICR CLG-RAVI KUMAR` → clearing tag instead of name**
The name-extraction rule for `CHQ PAID-` used `sep_by_dash(desc,3,4)`, which targets the 4-segment layout. This 3-segment layout put the name at index 2, so index 3 was empty.
Fix (`HDFC.py`): added a rule for the 3-segment `CHQ PAID-MICR CLG-` layout (`count('-')==2`) that lifts segment 2, placed ahead of the existing rule so the 4-segment layout is untouched.

**F-3 — national-transfer (NEFT) credit → blank name**
Line 228 unconditionally blanked any NEFT `partyName` matching a numeric ref shape. On the layout where the remitter ref sits in the segment *ahead* of the name, this discarded a recoverable name.
Fix (`HDFC.py`): before the blanking rule, if the following segment contains alphabetic text (a real name), use it; otherwise keep blanking. The guard fixture with no trailing name still blanks correctly.

**F-4 — instant-payment (UPI) credit → bare reference number**
The numeric-handle fallback (`[0-9]{9,15}` → segment 2) surfaced segment 2 verbatim even when segment 2 was itself a bare reference (`upi123456789`).
Fix (`HDFC.py`): keep the fallback when segment 2 is a real VPA (contains `@`); suppress it (leave blank) when segment 2 is a bare reference shaped as short-alpha-prefix + digits; otherwise behave as before.

**F-5 — penny-drop "is this account live" credit → `TRANSFER`**
HDFC's verification detection used the shared `BANK_VER`, which misses the bare slash-token markers (`/bav`, `/bene`, `/BV1`, `/PENNY D`, `/AC VALI`) that other banks (SBI, BOI) already treat as verification.
Fix: pointed HDFC's rule at the HDFC-specific `BANK_VER_HD` and added those markers to it (anchored with `\b` so `/bav` can't fire mid-word, e.g. "bavarian"). Isolating on `BANK_VER_HD` leaves Axis/BOM/Indusind, which share `BANK_VER`, unaffected.

## Verification
- All 5 fixtures derive baseline; the pre-fix run confirmed all 5 failed first.
- Pre-fix vs post-fix diff over 100 rows (findings + near-neighbours + ordinary traffic, both SAVING and CURRENT): **only the intended rows changed** — every other row keeps its exact category and name.
- Guard rows verified to stay put: NEFT ref-only credits still blank, ordinary UPI VPAs/names resolve, `Y/AC VALIDATION` still recognized, `SALARY/bavarian food` no longer misfires as verification, 5- and 7-digit debits stay `TRANSFER`.

The harness/corpus/runner live in `/tmp/opencode/` (`harness.py`, `neighbours.py`, `regression.py`, `dump.py`) if you want to re-run the acceptance check.
